text: The patient is positioned supine on the operating table. Three laparoscopic ports are visible: a 10mm structural balloon cannula below the umbilicus, and two 5mm cannulae in the mid-hypogastric and suprapubic regions. The surgeon and assistant are positioned on opposite sides of the table.
The surgeon makes an incision beneath the umbilicus, extending down to the rectus fascia, initiating the dissection process.

The surgeon divides the anterior rectus fascia and inserts a balloon dissector beneath the right rectus muscle and posterior rectus fascia. The dissector is advanced to the pubic symphysis, expanded, and then replaced with a structural balloon cannula.
The ten millimeter thirty degree laparoscope is positioned in the insufflated preperitoneal space, maintained at a pressure of eleven millimeters of mercury to avoid subcutaneous emphysema.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection with the laparoscope to identify anatomical features, focusing on the linea alba. The initial five millimeter cannula is placed in the suprapubic position, revealing the left and right rectus muscles.
The surgeon inserts a five-millimeter cannula into the preperitoneal space through the linea alba, with the left rectus muscle visible in the surgical field.

The laparoscope is positioned just to the right of the linea alba as dissection begins. The inferior epigastric artery and vein are visible on the right side of the screen, while the surgeon carefully dissects to expose Cooper's ligament.

The surgeon carefully defines the anatomy of the iliac fossa, revealing the right-sided Cooper's ligament. Gentle sweeping motions are used to avoid avulsing blood vessels, with one instrument providing retraction. The iliopubic tract is visible horizontally alongside Cooper's ligament.
The surgeon uses two graspers as blunt dissectors, with one retracting upward and the other pulling downward. A direct hernia becomes visible just medial to the epigastric vessels.

The surgeon uses a grasper to hold the abdominal wall up while another grasper gently pulls downward, manipulating preperitoneal fat that appears to be herniating into the direct space. The tissue resistance is visually evident as the graspers interact with the abdominal structures.
The camera view shows the epigastric vessels or their branches positioned over the lateral tube, just above the direct space. Cooper's ligament remains visible below these vessels.

The surgeon dissects down Cooper's ligament, revealing the iliac vein as the lateral-most structure visible in the surgical field. The dissection is halted at the iliac vein to prevent injury, emphasizing the importance of avoiding further lateral dissection.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection near Cooper's ligament, stopping at the iliac vein. A noticeable fullness is observed, exhibiting respiratory variations, as indicated by the surgeon's pointing.

The surgeon dissects between Cooper's ligament and the iliopubic tract, revealing the femoral space. The iliac vein is clearly visible, located laterally, with the iliac artery positioned slightly more lateral. The area where a femoral hernia would appear is just medial to the iliac vein.

The surgeon observes a fatty-looking mass, potentially a small femoral hernia, in the surgical field of a forty-year-old male patient. Careful dissection is performed around the mass to assess its characteristics and relation to surrounding structures.

The surgeon encounters resistance while attempting to dissect a structure just medial to the iliac vein. Upon closer inspection, it is identified as an iliac lymph node rather than herniated fat. The surgeon continues to examine the area to ensure there is no femoral hernia present.

The surgeon removes preperitoneal fat from the roof of the dissection area to prevent interference with mesh placement. Cooper's ligament and the iliac vein are visible, and the dissection progresses from medial to lateral, revealing the iliopubic tract.

The surgeon is positioned just above the iliopubic tract, identifying a small direct hernia in this area. Preperitoneal fat is visible and is being separated to minimize blood loss. The inferior epigastric artery and vein are seen, marking the boundary between the deep inguinal ring and the direct space, as the surgeon works to clarify the anatomical structures.

The surgeon carefully removes preperitoneal fat from the iliac fossa to prevent mesh crinkling during placement. The iliac vein is clearly visible, and dissection proceeds laterally over the iliac artery. The surgeon exercises caution to avoid bleeding from small branches off the major vessels.

The surgeon uses two graspers to carefully dissect near vascular structures, with one grasper stabilizing the blood vessel while the other pulls downward to separate surrounding tissue, avoiding direct traction on the vessel.

The surgeon identifies the edge of the peritoneum, which is adherent to the spermatic cord. The peritoneum is clearly visible as it is dissected down, revealing the structures of the spermatic cord. The surgeon carefully uses a grasper to avoid crushing the blood vessels within the spermatic cord.

The surgeon identifies the vas deferens, a bright white structure of the spermatic cord, with the peritoneal membrane visible below. The surgeon carefully pulls the peritoneum down while avoiding grasping the spermatic cord, searching for the associated blood vessels within the mass of fat. The dissector is maneuvered behind the spermatic cord, revealing vascular structures running with the fat. The peritoneum is peeled downward, illustrating the typical appearance of an indirect hernia as it would climb up the cord through the deep inguinal ring.

The surgeon carefully peels preperitoneal fat adherent to the right iliac fossa, taking care to avoid injuring the sensory nerves, including the iliohypogastric, ilioinguinal, genitofemoral, and lateral femoral cutaneous nerves, which run in the iliac fossa wall. The dissection clarifies the anatomy by separating the sac and fat.

The surgeon examines the peritoneum, noting its adherence to the spermatic cord without passing through the deep inguinal ring, indicating it is not a true indirect inguinal hernia. A small direct hernia is identified, but no additional significant findings are observed to explain the patient's bilateral inguinal hernias.

The surgeon identifies the iliac vein and artery, with the spermatic cord positioned over the iliac artery. Fat is carefully peeled down to ensure the mesh repair can hang unobstructed. The femoral space is examined, revealing a lymph node and the iliopubic tract, also known as Poupart's ligament. A small direct hernia is visible, correlating with earlier findings. The dissection of the right iliac fossa is completed, and the surgeon proceeds to perform a similar dissection on the left iliac fossa.

The surgeon identifies Cooper's ligament on the left side, using gentle sweeping motions to avoid avulsing small blood vessels. The preperitoneal fat is pulled down from Cooper's ligament, revealing vascular structures, including the epigastric vein and possibly the epigastric artery, as the dissection continues laterally towards the iliac vein.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the spermatic cord on the left side, visible along with the inferior epigastric vessels at the upper part of the screen. Using graspers, the surgeon encircles the cord to examine it thoroughly, ensuring no hernia sac or excess preperitoneal fat remains attached.

The surgeon carefully dissects the peritoneum from the spermatic cord, revealing preperitoneal fat that was not initially obvious. The peritoneum is positioned in the preperitoneal space, not protruding through the deep inguinal ring. The cord structures are visible to the right, with no significant hernia present.

The surgeon examines the spermatic cord near the deep ring, identifying preperitoneal fat lateral to the cord. Gently pulling on the fat, a sizable piece is extracted from the inguinal canal, potentially explaining the patient's symptomatic hernia on the left side.

The surgeon returns to the right side, examining the area near the deep ring for preperitoneal fat. Arterial pulsations are visible through the fat, indicating the proximity of the iliac artery. Careful traction reveals preperitoneal fat extending into the inguinal canal, similar to the findings on the left side, explaining the patient's bilateral inguinal bulges.

The surgeon carefully peels away preperitoneal fat from the deep inguinal ring, revealing the iliac artery and surrounding lymph nodes. The fat appears somewhat bloody, suggesting embedded lymph nodes. The major piece of fat is successfully removed from the deep inguinal ring, allowing for mesh placement to prevent recurrence.

The surgeon inspects the now empty deep inguinal ring after reducing preperitoneal fat bilaterally. Preparation is underway to place polypropylene mesh in the preperitoneal space beneath the inguinal canal on both sides.

The surgeon introduces a six by four inch piece of medium weight polypropylene mesh into the preperitoneal space. Initially, the mesh is turned ninety degrees, requiring adjustment to achieve its intended open configuration. The mesh's memory allows it to be manipulated into the correct orientation.

The surgeon maneuvers the rectangular polypropylene mesh into the preperitoneal space, ensuring the long axis is oriented deep into the pelvis and the short axis is positioned sideways. The mesh is laid flat without slitting, following thorough dissection.

The surgeon reorients the mesh by turning it ninety degrees and positions it underneath the inguinal canal. The mesh is adjusted to hang loosely without folding, suspended by tacks placed into Cooper's ligament and the muscle above. The tacks are carefully positioned above the iliopubic tract.

The surgeon positions the upper edge of the polypropylene mesh to cover the small direct hernia, despite the primary presence of an indirect hernia. The tacker is used to secure the mesh, ensuring it is placed above the iliopubic tract, with tacks potentially placed into Cooper's ligament.

The surgeon uses absorbable polyglycolic acid tacks to secure the mesh, starting with the first tack placed into Cooper's ligament. The mesh is flattened and adjusted to ensure it lays properly, covering the direct hernia defect. The surgeon continues to adjust the mesh to achieve optimal coverage.

The surgeon uses a tacker as a blunt dissector to maneuver the mesh, ensuring it overlaps the hernia rather than filling it. Adhesions are freed by sweeping behind the mesh, allowing it to be positioned correctly. Counter pressure is applied to the abdominal wall while placing tacks along the superior edge of the mesh, including a final tack into Cooper's ligament. The mesh hangs smoothly without crimping at the bottom.

The surgeon completes the placement of the mesh, ensuring it covers the deep ring and direct space on the right side. The mesh is maneuvered into position on the left side, where it is tacked along Cooper's ligament. The indirect preperitoneal fat hernia is addressed, with the direct space covered effectively.
